exact_primitives: Seller capacity is privately high or low each period
Only a high-capacity seller may buy a truthful public certificate at cost k
A certificate is removed when one buyer trades with that seller
A buyer may inspect one uncertified seller at cost c, buy without inspection, or exit
benchmarks.label: Focal consumable-stock environment
benchmarks.prediction: The focal mapping from k to inspection, allocative efficiency, and buyer surplus is left unresolved.
benchmarks.solution_concept: An equilibrium concept for the dynamic Bayesian game, to be specified before analysis.
timing: Eligible sellers choose whether to certify
Buyer observes certificates and chooses inspection, purchase, or exit
A trade consumes that seller's certificate
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: This is the reference environment; no benchmark comparison or sign is asserted.

exact_primitives: A truthful certificate remains publicly visible after a buyer trades
A certificate is not removed by a sale
benchmarks.label: Non-consumable certificate benchmark
benchmarks.prediction: This benchmark isolates the effect of changing k when the trade-triggered depletion of public certificates is removed.
benchmarks.solution_concept: The same equilibrium concept as the focal environment, once specified.
timing: Sellers observe capacity and eligible sellers may certify
Buyer chooses inspection, purchase, or exit
A trade does not deplete certificate visibility
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: Any difference from the focal environment is attributable to removing trade-triggered certificate consumption, subject to the common equilibrium selection.

exact_primitives: All focal primitives are retained except the posterior distribution of capacity conditional on an uncertified seller is fixed at a k-invariant value
The fixed uncertified posterior is used in the buyer's inspection payoff calculation
benchmarks.label: Fixed uncertified-pool composition benchmark
benchmarks.prediction: This benchmark removes the composition response of the uncertified pool while retaining the comparison over k.
benchmarks.solution_concept: The focal buyer-choice rule with the uncertified-pool posterior treated as an imposed primitive.
timing: The buyer evaluates an uncertified seller using the fixed posterior
The buyer chooses inspection, purchase, or exit
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: Any difference from the focal environment isolates the endogenous-composition component rather than a mechanical change in k alone.

exact_primitives: All focal seller and certificate primitives are retained
The buyer's inspection action is fixed exogenously before certificates and capacities are observed
The fixed inspection rule does not respond to k or the uncertified-pool posterior
benchmarks.label: Exogenous inspection benchmark
benchmarks.prediction: This benchmark removes the feedback from certificate availability and uncertified-pool composition to the buyer's choice to inspect.
benchmarks.solution_concept: The seller-side dynamic game coupled with the imposed inspection rule.
timing: An exogenous inspection rule is applied
Sellers observe capacity and eligible sellers may certify
Buyer trade or exit follows the imposed inspection rule and observed information
A trade consumes a certificate in the same way as the focal environment
benchmarks.unresolved_delta: Any difference from the focal environment isolates the endogenous-inspection margin, without asserting that a difference occurs.

payload.exact_question_delta: The benchmarks hold the basic two-seller private-capacity environment fixed while separately deleting certificate consumption, freezing the informational composition of the uncertified pool, or removing the buyer's endogenous inspection choice; they therefore distinguish the proposed consumable-stock channel from cheaper certification alone.

entities.summary: Proposed benchmarks isolate certificate consumption, uncertified-pool composition, and endogenous inspection from a mechanical reduction in certification cost.
entities.title: Benchmarks for consumable certificate stocks

payload.rationale: The economic mechanism and separating benchmarks are concrete enough for human review, but the scope retains unresolved equilibrium-selection and benchmark-implementation choices. This dossier proposes no confirmation and records the kill condition and scope risks for the human G1 decision.

requirements.description: The question specifies the k perturbation, the focal outcome mappings, and the unresolved consumable-stock mechanism.
The benchmark set separately removes trade-triggered certificate consumption, freezes the uncertified posterior, and makes inspection exogenous.
Equilibrium selection, belief updating, and the imposed value used in the fixed-composition benchmark remain unspecified and may require narrowing before analysis.
The framing should be killed if consumption and the induced uncertified posterior are payoff-irrelevant or all candidate patterns survive each channel-removing benchmark unchanged.

entities.summary: A non-confirming dossier for a later human decision on whether to retain, revise, narrow, or kill the proposed question and benchmark scope.
entities.title: Proposed G1 dossier: consumable certificate question and benchmarks

edges.economic_meaning: k changes the private cost of the truthful certification action.
Issued certificates form the publicly visible stock.
A trade depletes a visible certificate in the focal environment.
The stock and its depletion can change which sellers remain uncertified.
The uncertain seller composition changes the return to paying c for inspection.
Inspection is one route into the buyer's trade or exit choice.
The proposed equilibrium maps the primitives into the focal outcomes.

nodes.economic_meaning: Sellers internalize discounted future margins and hold private current capacity information.
nodes.label: Two long-lived sellers

nodes.economic_meaning: Each buyer makes a one-period information-acquisition and trade decision.
nodes.label: Short-lived buyer sequence

nodes.economic_meaning: A seller privately knows whether current service capacity is high or low before certification and buyer choice.
nodes.label: Private high or low capacity

nodes.economic_meaning: The proposed comparative-static perturbation changes the cost faced by an eligible high-capacity seller for a truthful certificate.
nodes.label: Certificate cost k
primitive_sufficient_conditions: k varies while the stated capacity distribution, c, buyer value, margin, and discounting are held fixed.

nodes.economic_meaning: Only a high-capacity seller can purchase the public certificate, so certification can sort current capacity.
nodes.label: Truthful certificate eligibility
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Common to all listed benchmarks except where certificate persistence changes.

nodes.economic_meaning: Certificate visibility is a state variable because a sale removes the certificate and changes future public information.
nodes.label: Trade-consumed certificate stock
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Differs from bm_no_certificate_consumption, where a sale does not remove visibility.

nodes.economic_meaning: The posterior probability that an uncertified seller is high determines the informational return to inspection and may respond to certification and depletion.
nodes.label: Uncertified-pool composition
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Differs from bm_fixed_uncertified_composition, where this posterior is imposed and k-invariant.

nodes.economic_meaning: The buyer may pay c to inspect one uncertified seller rather than buy without inspecting or exit.
nodes.label: Endogenous buyer inspection
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Differs from bm_exogenous_inspection, where the inspection rule is fixed and does not respond to k or the posterior.

nodes.economic_meaning: Buyer action determines service realization, seller margin, certificate consumption, and the realized buyer payoff.
nodes.label: Trade, uninspected purchase, or exit
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Common action set apart from the imposed inspection rule in bm_exogenous_inspection.

nodes.economic_meaning: A successful service gives buyer value one and a successful trade gives the seller a margin; sellers discount future profits.
nodes.label: Service value and seller margin
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Normalizations held common across all listed benchmarks.

nodes.economic_meaning: Strategies and beliefs must jointly determine certification, inspection, trade, and the certificate-stock transition.
nodes.label: Dynamic Bayesian equilibrium candidate
primitive_sufficient_conditions: The exact equilibrium concept and selection are not yet specified.

nodes.economic_meaning: These are the outcomes whose mapping from k is proposed for study, without a claimed direction.
nodes.label: Inspection, allocation, and buyer surplus
primitive_sufficient_conditions: No non-monotonicity or welfare result is asserted.

entities.summary: A proposed decomposition of the information, timing, choice, and dynamic feedback primitives in the framed question.
entities.title: Primitives of the consumable certificate environment

payload.importance: The question distinguishes a lower certification cost as a contemporaneous price incentive from a dynamic information-supply change that can alter what remains hidden and what buyers learn by paying to inspect.
payload.kill_condition: Kill this framing if, after an equilibrium selection is specified, certificate consumption and the induced uncertified-pool posterior are payoff-irrelevant for every admissible k, or if every candidate pattern in inspection, allocative efficiency, and buyer surplus is unchanged in all benchmarks that remove consumption, freeze the uncertified-pool composition, or make inspection exogenous.
payload.object_to_explain: Holding the capacity distribution, buyer value, seller margin, discounting, and search cost c fixed, how the equilibrium correspondence maps certificate cost k into buyer inspection, ex ante allocative efficiency, and buyer surplus once certificate issuance, stock depletion, uncertified-pool composition, and inspection are jointly endogenous.
payload.phenomenon: When truthful certificates are consumed by trade, the outstanding certificate stock can change both the composition of sellers who remain uncertified and the payoff to inspecting an uncertified seller.
prohibited_claims: No direction of any comparative static is asserted.
No novelty, proof, validation, or welfare result is asserted.
No human G1 question or benchmark decision is asserted.
payload.proposed_scope: Two long-lived sellers and an infinite sequence of short-lived buyers; sellers privately observe high or low current capacity; only a high-capacity seller can buy a truthful certificate at cost k; a certificate remains public until one buyer trades with that seller; buyers may inspect one uncertified seller at cost c, buy without inspecting, or exit. This is a proposed framing only and does not select an equilibrium, solve the model, or assert a comparative-static direction.
payload.unresolved_delta: It is unresolved whether any of these mappings is non-monotone in k, whether any such pattern exists, or whether the consumption-and-composition channel is necessary for it.

entities.summary: A proposed question about whether the state-dependent depletion of truthful certificates can alter the comparative-static pattern of buyer behavior and outcomes as certificate cost changes.
entities.title: Consumable certificate stock and buyer inspection

content.authority: This route may explore and propose at L1. It cannot create human confirmation or alter a confirmed scientific scope.
content.budget_estimator: etai_lexical_v1 is deterministic lexical accounting, not a provider token count.
content.scope: Preserve exact assumptions, quantifiers, scope, dissent, and stale blockers. Generated context and candidates are noncanonical.
content.validity: Formal truth, economic interpretation, literature novelty, human acceptance, and freshness are distinct.

instructions: Audit the exact current ResearchQuestion, BenchmarkSet, PrimitiveGraph, and G1 GateDossier for economic meaning before any human G1 decision.
An optional current FramingQualityBundle predecessor is admissible only for continued diagnosis over that identical exact upstream scope and must be included in the audit context when present.

Produce one FramingQualityBundle: create it under a new entity ID at version 1 for a first audit or any new upstream lineage, and supersede only that exact optional predecessor when continuing diagnosis.
Always create the strengthened replacement G1 GateDossier under a new entity ID at version 1; never supersede or mutate any GateDossier.
The replacement dossier's ordered evidence must include and be governed by the resulting bundle, and the pre-audit dossier remains historical evidence that cannot support a current G1 action.

Explain the economic puzzle, the relevant opposing force or other archetype-appropriate tension, the active causal chain, the stakes, one explicitly illustrative minimal example, the nearby benchmark, and the kill condition in language an economist can accurately retell.
Before calling any choice-dependent mechanism link active, run an active-margin payoff check.
In one concrete state, compare the claimed response with a distinct feasible alternative under the same timing, information, beliefs, and continuation convention.
For a binary action set, state why those two actions exhaust the feasible set.

For a multi-action or continuous choice, compare against the best feasible deviation, a deviation payoff envelope, or the economically appropriate local variation rather than a convenient single alternative.
Give continuation-inclusive payoffs, state the necessary best-response, mixing, or local optimality condition, and decide whether its admissible solution region is nonempty.

The status_basis must explain how the upstream source of this link changes the payoff difference, deviation envelope, or local incentive; if that comparative response is not established, classify the margin as unresolved.
Run the dominance check before writing the mechanism prose: a verbal "may" is not a payoff witness, and an action that is dominated throughout the exact proposed ledger is inactive.

Every active-margin witness must include one consequence_binding. Bind the witnessed decision to an exact ordered PrimitiveGraph edge path, the causal consequence it changes, the transition kind, and typed conditions defining the relevant public-state class. The focal and best alternative actions must produce different consequences at that same state. A decreasing transition cannot be witnessed from a zero consequence state. Do not use a state in which the claimed transition is infeasible merely because another action margin is active there.

A downstream technological transition may remain unwitnessed when its exact path introduces no new choice, but a mechanical arrow that relies on an upstream choice is active only if that operative choice has a payoff witness somewhere in the chain.
Give every witness a link-specific kill condition.

If a claimed choice link is inactive, disclose a causal-attribution defect, propose revise_framing with an exact upstream repair target, and remove or downgrade that link; do not relabel a missing payoff tradeoff as a later scope choice.
If activity remains unresolved, continue diagnosis or revise rather than propose G1 readiness.

Audit every benchmark exactly once: distinguish what changes, what is held fixed and at which level, what reoptimizes, what remains endogenous, which active path reaches each target outcome, and whether the attribution survives equilibrium-selection concerns.

Every benchmark assessment must explicitly declare distinctive_mechanism as not_claimed, unresolved, choice_mediated, or mechanical_transition. Also set distinctive_mechanism_contribution_status once for the paper-level claim: claimed means at least one exact active benchmark-distinctive mechanism; not_claimed means every row honestly disclaims mechanism absence as a contribution and remains admissible when benchmarks share the same mechanism; unresolved means at least one such distinction remains unresolved and cannot support readiness. When a focal benchmark claims a mechanism absent from a contrast, name that exact contrast and bind the distinctive primitive or state nodes, transition edges, downstream consequence, transition kind, and required public-state conditions. The focal path must contain this exact spine, and at least one declared node or transition must be absent from the contrast path. Identical focal and contrast paths cannot support a distinctive stock, depletion, information, or other mechanism claim.

For a choice_mediated distinctive mechanism, one active payoff witness must have a consequence_binding matching the claim's exact consequence, transition edges, transition kind, and public-state conditions. A payoff comparison from another state or another downstream consequence is not evidence for this claim. For a mechanical_transition, the distinctive spine may omit a payoff witness only when its declared distinctive nodes and transition edges introduce no choice; do not hide a choice inside a mechanical label.

If the distinctive spine or its relevant state cannot yet be established, mark the claim unresolved, disclose a benchmark-linked causal-attribution gap, and propose revise_framing. Do not repair a missing spine by repeating the same generic path in both focal and contrast rows.
Holding a pointwise policy fixed does not hold its aggregate fixed when its weighting distribution or transition remains endogenous.
A frozen ledger with no active response margin is not a mechanism control.
A fixed selector is a reproducible convention, not selection robustness.
Do not repair or reinterpret a false upstream object inside the audit; disclose the defect, downgrade the attribution, and propose revision or continued diagnosis.

Good prose cannot compensate for an unsupported aggregate invariance claim, a placebo control, an inactive payoff margin, an unbound distinctive mechanism, an infeasible consequence state, or unsupported selection robustness.
The route may propose readiness and supply evidence, but it must never record, confirm, or imply an effective G1 decision; only the human researcher can confirm G1.
